title: 4.10 Heap
subtitle: Heap structure, priority queue operations, and heapify

content.introduction: Heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies a global order constraint between parent and child nodes. It is a core structure for priority queues, task scheduling, top-k queries, and graph algorithms like Dijkstra and Prim.

sections.title: 4.10.1 Definition and Core Properties
sections.content:
A heap is a complete binary tree:
1) All levels except possibly the last are full.
2) Last level is filled from left to right.

Heap-order property:
- Max-heap: parent >= children.
- Min-heap: parent <= children.

Because of completeness, heap is naturally stored in an array and does not require explicit pointers.
examples: Max-heap root is always the current maximum
Min-heap root is always the current minimum
Complete-tree shape makes index-based navigation possible

sections.title: 4.10.2 Array Representation and Index Rules
sections.content:
For 0-based arrays:
- Parent index of i: (i - 1) / 2 
- Left child of i: 2i + 1
- Right child of i: 2i + 2

Advantages:
- O(1) access to parent and children by index formula.
- Better cache locality than pointer-based trees.

Boundary checks are essential when child index exceeds array length.
examples: Node at index 3 has parent index 1
Node at index 2 has children indices 5 and 6

sections.title: 4.10.3 Fundamental Heap Operations
sections.content:
1) Insert (push): append element to tail, then sift-up until heap property is restored.
2) Extract-top (pop): remove root, move last element to root, then sift-down.
3) Peek: read root directly.
4) Heapify build: build heap from an unsorted array bottom-up.

Complexities:
- Insert: O(log n)
- Extract-top: O(log n)
- Peek: O(1)
- Build heap (bottom-up): O(n)
examples: Insert 60 into max-heap and move it upward by swaps
Pop max value then sink replacement element down
Build heap faster with bottom-up heapify than repeated insert

sections.title: 4.10.4 Typical Applications and Comparison
sections.content:
Typical scenarios:
- Priority queues (task scheduling, event simulation).
- Top-k and streaming extremes.
- Heap sort.
- Support structures in Dijkstra/Prim.

Comparison notes:
- Compared with balanced BST, heap gives faster top access but does not support ordered traversal efficiently.
- Compared with unsorted arrays, heap significantly improves repeated top extraction.
examples: Real-time scheduler picks highest-priority job from heap
Top-100 hot items maintained with a fixed-size min-heap
Heap sort uses max-heap to produce ascending order

keyTakeaways: Heap combines complete-tree shape with parent-child order constraints
Array representation makes heap operations simple and efficient
Sift-up and sift-down are the core maintenance procedures
Build-heap can be done in linear time O(n)
Heap is the standard implementation base for priority queues
